messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: Oddly, I may be seeing the same (in Bluntisham, Cambs) but a bit too early to tell as I only moved mine higher at the weekend. My repeater only has two neighbours since the move whereas before I had four. I was expecting an improvement given it is now ~9m above ground level and has 8.5 dBi antenna rather than loft at 5m with 6.5 drive as it was previously. My theory is that I am now receiving more noise too and it may be working against me. I'll try verify my theory after Christmas using a cheap RTL SDR dongle and the decide whether there is any noise and whether it is in-band or out-band. If the latter, I'll buy a cavity filter. (edited)

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. No internet routing, no messing with RF, nothing in the message path. It just listens, observes, and feeds back what already happened. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. If you fancy a look: https://meshrank.net Happy to answer questions or take criticism

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)
messages.content_html: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)

messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: I added an Asacom cavity filter to my setup last night. In the previous month I averaged 3,700 received packets per day, as shown above. In the last 23 hours I have received an additional 12,500 packets! Noise floor has dropped from -107 to -118 dBm. I turned on auto adding of contacts. My list increase from ~120 to the maximum 350 over night. It's a bit early to tell if this is fluke or sustained, but it looks promising! Do note that I am in close proximity to water towers (<200m) with multiple cellular networks on it, and it is those that I wanted to try filter. If you're not in a noisy environment, a filter will not improve anything, and in fact it will degrade sensitivity by the insertion loss.

messages.text: You have to manually account for the RX LNA gain on V4 because software doesn't. I don't know what the gain is but most LNA are in the 15 to 20 dB range which would align. I think it is rare that we are sensitivity limited and it is more usual that we are SNR limited. LoRa can only pull signals up to 20 dB below the noise floor. An LNA will not reduce external noise.

messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: Exactly. Both signal and noise and amplified by the LNA. The LNA also adds its own noise to the signal. For the V4 this is about 2 dB - a bit crap really when you can readily get much better. The ones I used on my designs for cellular based stations were 0.35 dB. That's the performance vs price trade off. The V4 seems a bit of an odd product, especially outside of USA. I'm not sure the receive performance is better than the V3, and suspect it could be worse in areas with high levels of noise without external filtering. And the ~1W (29 dBm) TX is limited usefulness because non-amplfied (22 dBm) repeaters with 7 dBi antenna gain already hit the legal limits of 29 dBm EIRP. The extra power is useful for handheld devices with <2 dBi antenna gain though. (edited)

messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: From the Semtech SX1262 datasheet table 6-1. SF Recoverable Below Noise SF7 ~ –7.5 dB SF8 ~ –10 dB SF9 ~ –12.5 dB SF10 ~ –15 dB SF11 ~ –17.5 dB SF12 ~ –20 dB We use SF8 so we can recover a signal 10 dB below noise floor. Previously when my noise was -107 dBm this meant a sensitivity of -117 dBm. Now with a noise floor of -117 I can recover signals down to -127 dBm. In perfect conditions (space, free of obstacles) each 6 dB is a doubling of range. Real world is usually less because we are usually line of sight limited. (edited)

messages.text: Have you guys seen https://re.jrc.ec.europa.eu/pvg_tools/en/ . It has a great tool for off-grid solar energy calculations. messages.author: Dain Bramage
messages.text: Is the Lincoln Cathedral node up and running? Down here in Cambridgeshire Fens we have a problem with DD TOP and to a lesser extent DDRC in that we receive thousands of messages from them but are unable to reply. I wondered if they are either in a very prominent location, meaning signals can space easily but they may also suffer from a high noise floor, preventing reception of weak signals. Or perhaps they are blasting a lot of power. Any issues with tx back to cathedral from the peripherals of Lincolnshire?
messages.content_html: Is the Lincoln Cathedral node up and running? I wondered if they are either in a very prominent location, meaning signals can space easily but they may also suffer from a high noise floor, preventing reception of weak signals.
